senses.definition: to slap one's palm against the palm of another person as a way of greeting or congratulating them

examples.x: Delighted, I gave him some skin, and his mother gave him a big hug.
It's always nice to see old friends and give them some skin as a warm welcome.

senses.definition: to have the opportunity to spend time with a famous person, whether professionally or socially

examples.x: The gala was an excellent opportunity to rub shoulders with celebrities and philanthropists.

senses.definition: to enjoy a friendly talk with a person for a long period of time

examples.x: We spent the evening chewing the fat about our favorite movies and music.
After the game, the teammates gathered at the bar to chew the fat and celebrate their victory.
Old friends enjoy getting together to chew the fat and reminisce about the good old days.
During the long road trip, they chewed the fat to pass the time and share travel stories.
At family gatherings, we often sit around the table and chew the fat over dinner, discussing various topics.

senses.definition: to keep talking with a person about no particular subject

examples.x: We've been shooting the breeze for well over an hour now.

senses.definition: to make two or more strangers get comfortable with each other and engage in a conversation

examples.x: He told a lighthearted joke to break the ice at the beginning of the meeting.
Starting a conversation about common interests can help break the ice at a social event.
In a new workplace, team-building activities are often used to break the ice among colleagues.
Sometimes a simple smile and a friendly greeting are all it takes to break the ice with a stranger.
At the party, she played a game that encouraged guests to interact and break the ice.

senses.definition: used for greeting a person whom one has not seen for a significant period of time

examples.x: After a year abroad, I returned to my hometown, and my friends welcomed me with "hello stranger."

senses.definition: brief and polite conversation about random subjects, often in a social setting

examples.x: During the reception, they engaged in small talk about the weather and local events.
When you meet someone for the first time, it's common to start with some small talk to get to know each other.
In an elevator ride, small talk often revolves around comments about the day or the building.
During the coffee break, the coworkers engaged in small talk to relax and unwind.
Small talk can be a useful skill for networking and building relationships in social and professional settings.

senses.definition: someone or something that when seen makes one very pleased

examples.x: The sunset over the Strait of Malacca is a sight for sore eyes.
After a month of traveling, my cozy home was a sight for sore eyes.
Seeing her smiling face at the airport was a sight for sore eyes after being apart for so long.
The beautiful sunset over the ocean was a sight for sore eyes after a day of hard work.

senses.definition: used as a question when one wants to know what another person has in mind at the moment

examples.x: While they were sitting in silence, he suddenly said, 'A penny for your thoughts?'

senses.definition: said when one is in the middle of a conversation about a particular person and that person suddenly shows up

examples.x: I was telling Jane about your recent success, and you walked in ‚Äď talk of the devil.
We were just talking about you, and here you are ‚Äď speak of the devil!
Speak of the devil!
We were talking about the new project lead, and she appeared ‚Äď talk of the devil.
I mentioned your name, and suddenly, you walked through the door ‚Äď speak of the devil!

senses.definition: used to ask a departing person to keep visiting or communicating with one

examples.x: It's been a while since we last met; please don't be a stranger ‚Äď let's catch up soon.
When a close friend moves away, we often say, 'Don't be a stranger,' to convey our desire to stay connected.

senses.definition: (particularly of a guest) to stay in someone's house longer than is considered appropriate, often to the point where one is not welcomed anymore

examples.x: After the cool reception I received at breakfast, it was apparent that I had overstayed my welcome at the cottage of my father's friend.
After the kindness that had been shown to him, he didn't want to outstay his welcome.
As much as he has contributed to the company, he has outstayed his welcome and needs to go.
We had a great time at the party, but we didn't want to overstay our welcome, so we left before midnight.
After the weekend stay with my friends, I knew it was time to go home; I didn't want to overstay my welcome.

senses.definition: to compeletly end one's relationship with someone

examples.x: When her son was arrested again she washed her hands of him.

senses.definition: to choose not to communicate or interact with other people

examples.x: She's a quiet person who tends to keep to herself, especially in a crowded room.
Despite being in a social environment, he chose to keep to himself and read a book.
Some people find solace in nature and prefer to keep to themselves while hiking in the wilderness.
